1: The divine-speech formula vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor introduces two distinct units: the lamp and bread instructions (vv. 2-9) and the blasphemer case (vv. 10-23). The juxtaposition is structural — the perpetual presence of light and bread in the sanctuary (the ongoing covenant relationship) is immediately followed by a case-law that tests the covenant's protection of the divine Name.
2: Shemen zayit zakh katit (zakh, H2134; katit, H3795): 'pure pressed olive oil.' Zakh = clear, pure, unadulterated; katit = beaten/crushed in a mortar (not extracted by pressing the whole olive, but from olives beaten to release oil without bitter residue). Ner tamid (tamid, H8548): 'perpetual/continual lamp' — tamid means unbroken continuity, not necessarily a flame never extinguished but one always maintained in attendance. The same adjective qualifies the daily burnt offering ('olat ha-tamid) and the bread of presence — it marks the perpetual character of covenant worship.
3: Lifne YHWH tamid: 'before YHWH continually.' Aaron tends the lamp from evening to morning — the light burns through the night (when natural light is absent) before YHWH. Chuqat 'olam (H2708+H5769): perpetual statute — the same formula used for the Sabbath (Ex 31:16) and the fat-prohibition (Lev 3:17). The lamp's maintenance is not merely practical (lighting the sanctuary) but relational — a covenant obligation to keep the light of YHWH's presence burning.
4: Menorah ha-tahor: the pure (gold) lampstand. The lampstand's specification as 'pure' (tahor) refers to the refined gold of its construction (Ex 25:31). Aaron tends the lamps lifne YHWH tamid — the repeated phrase from v. 3 emphasizes that the lamp-tending is always before YHWH, not merely in the sacred space. The orientation of the priest's work is toward YHWH, not toward the congregation or any human audience.
5: Solet (H5560) fine flour for the bread; shetim 'esronim (two-tenths of an ephah) per loaf — approximately 4.4 liters of flour each. The twelve loaves correspond to the twelve tribes: the bread represents the totality of Israel's covenant community before YHWH. Challot (H2471): loaves or flat-cakes. The specific quantity per loaf and the total of twelve encode the theological numerology of the covenant: completeness of the people before the complete God.
6: Shisha ha-madreg: 'six in a row.' Two rows of six on the shulchan ha-tahor (the pure gold table, Ex 25:23-30). The table and bread together constitute the lechem ha-panim (H3899+H6440): 'bread of the face/presence' — bread that stands in the face-to-face space before YHWH. The term panim (face) echoes the promise of Presence in Ex 33:14-15 (panai yelekhu = 'my face will go'). The bread at YHWH's face is the covenant meal held in perpetuity.
7: Levonah zakkah (H3828+H2134): pure frankincense — a costly aromatic resin. It is placed on each row as an 'azkara (H234): memorial/representative portion. The frankincense is burned as the reah nihoah for YHWH; the bread itself is not burned but presented and then given to the priests (v. 9). The 'azkara structure here (frankincense = the burned representative) means the bread-rows are acceptable to YHWH through the frankincense memorial even though the bread itself goes to the priests.
8: Beyom ha-Shabbat: every Sabbath the bread is renewed — fresh loaves replace the previous week's. The renewal cycle on the Sabbath connects the bread of presence to the covenant's temporal rhythm: the bread is not a one-time offering but a perpetually renewed covenant act. Berit 'olam (H1285+H5769): 'covenant forever' — this specific phrase, linking the bread to the eternal covenant, elevates the showbread from a food-ritual to a covenant-sign. Compare Gen 9:16 (rainbow), 17:7 (circumcision), Ex 31:16 (Sabbath).
9: Qodesh qodashim: 'most holy' — the bread, though not burned, carries the highest holiness grade when eaten by the priests. Aaron and his sons eat it bimqom qadosh (in a holy place). Me'ishei YHWH chok 'olam: from YHWH's food offerings, a perpetual portion. The priestly consumption of the bread of presence is the completion of the covenant meal: YHWH presents (the bread before his face), and the priests receive and eat. The meal is conducted between YHWH and his mediating household.
10: Ben isha Israelit and ish Mitzri (Egyptian father): a man of mixed ancestry. This narrative interruption in the legal material highlights that the laws which follow apply across ethnic lines. The legal case emerges from a fight (vayinnatzu) in the camp — the blasphemy happened in a social conflict context, not in a cultic one. The blasphemer's identity (mixed heritage) sets up the ruling in v. 22 that the same law applies to the foreigner and the native-born.
11: Vayiqov (naqav, H5344): the specific verb for blasphemy of the Name — 'to pierce, to bore through, to profane by explicit use.' Naqav differs from the more common galah (to uncover/profane) or hillel (to desecrate): it implies a direct, specific use of the Name in a degrading or magical-manipulative way. Wayyeqalel (qalal, H7043): 'to curse, to treat as light/contemptible' — the opposite of kaved (to honor/weigh heavily). The blasphemy is both specific name-use (naqav) and contemptuous devaluation (qalal).
12: Lifros lahem al pi YHWH: 'until the matter was made clear to them through YHWH's mouth.' The custody pending divine ruling reflects the legal system's acknowledgment of a hard case: the community knows the act is serious but does not yet know the prescribed response for this specific crime. The consultation model — bringing exceptional cases to YHWH for ruling — parallels the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27) and the Passover exception (Num 9).
13: Second divine-speech formula (vayedabber YHWH) within the chapter — signaling that the following ruling on blasphemy is a direct divine legal decision, not Moses's inference from existing law. The narrative structure (case → consultation → divine ruling → execution → legal principle → execution) is the standard form for midrash-type legal development in the Torah.
14: The execution procedure for blasphemy: vesamkhu khol ha-shom'im et yedeihem al rosho — 'all who heard lay their hands on his head.' The witnesses become the first executioners — the laying of hands by witnesses reverses the semikhah of sacrifice (where the offerer's hand identifies with the animal). Here the witnesses' hands transfer the guilt back to the blasphemer before the community executes judgment. Venirgemuhu (H7275, H7275): stoning by the whole congregation.
15: Ish ish ki yeqalel Elohav venasa cheto: 'Any person who curses his God shall bear his guilt.' The formulation 'ish 'ish ('any man, any man' — an emphatic repetition in Hebrew law) universalizes the principle to every person without exception. Venasa cheto: 'shall bear his guilt/sin' — the language of guilt-bearing used across Leviticus for violations that fall on the transgressor himself. The passive form emphasizes that the guilt does not disappear but remains attached to the person.
16: Niqev shem YHWH mot yumat: 'Whoever pierces/blasphemes the name of YHWH shall be put to death.' The same naqav verb from v. 11. The death penalty applies to ger (resident alien) and ezrach (native-born) equally — the only verse in the Torah that explicitly applies the blasphemy law to non-Israelites. The equal application reflects the Name's absolute status: YHWH's name is not protected as Israel's tribal property but as the identity of the Creator God of all people.
17: Ki yakkeh kol nefesh adam mot yumat: 'Whoever strikes down any human being (nefesh adam) shall be put to death.' Nefesh (H5315): life-self, person, soul — here in the compound nefesh adam emphasizing the full personhood of the one killed. The term adam (human being, from adamah = ground) makes the protection of human life universal: not 'any Israelite' but 'any human being.' The life-protection law is grounded in the image of God (tselem Elohim, Gen 9:6) in which all humanity shares.
18: Makkeh nefesh behemah — killing an animal. Yeshallemenah nefesh tachat nefesh: 'he shall restore it — life for life.' The nefesh tachat nefesh formula applies here to animal restitution (monetary compensation, not execution), establishing that the same phrase can function at both capital (human) and property (animal) levels. The principle: equivalent compensation is owed; the nature of the victim determines the nature of the compensation.
19: Mum be'amito ken ye'aseh lo: 'as he has done to his neighbor, so it shall be done to him.' The lex talionis formula — introduced here before its detailed enumeration in v. 20. This general statement of equivalent retribution is the principle underlying all the specific applications that follow. Mum (H3971): a blemish, defect, or disfigurement — the same word used for physical disqualification from the priesthood (Lev 21:17-23). The legal concept of mum maps from the sacrificial system to personal injury law.
20: Shever tachat shever, 'ayin tachat 'ayin, shen tachat shen: 'fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth.' The lex talionis triad (also in Ex 21:24; Deut 19:21) is a principle of proportional justice, not a license for personal revenge. The formula limits punishment to the equivalent of the injury done — no more. In the ANE context, where wealthy offenders could simply pay fines and powerful ones could exact disproportionate retribution, the lex talionis was a leveling law: the punishment must fit the crime, regardless of relative social status.
21: The verse summarizes and connects both law categories: animal killing → restitution; human killing → execution. The juxtaposition (behemah... adam) makes the hierarchy explicit: human life carries capital weight; animal life carries restitution weight. The same nefesh term applies to both (both have life-essence), but the tselem Elohim of human beings uniquely grounds the capital response.
22: Mishpat echad yihyeh lachem: 'You shall have one law for you.' Lager kaeezrach: for the ger (resident alien) and the ezrach (native-born) alike. The legal egalitarianism of the Mosaic code — one standard across social and ethnic categories — is a defining feature repeatedly insisted upon (cf. Ex 12:49; Num 15:16,29). The covenant community is not a tribal law-zone where insiders are protected differently from outsiders; the law's authority extends to all who dwell in YHWH's land.
23: The blasphemer is brought outside the camp and stoned — Moses and the Israelites ka'asher tsivah YHWH et-Moshe ('as YHWH commanded Moses'). The narrative closure formula emphasizes exact obedience to the divine ruling: no deviation, no mercy plea. The legal case serves as a paradigm for all subsequent blasphemy cases: the procedure established here is the normative precedent. The outside-the-camp execution removes the guilty person and the pollution of blasphemy from the covenant community.
